adrs.title: Architecture Overview and System Design
adrs.summary: Adopts a layered modular architecture (Presentation / Application / Domain / Infrastructure) with DDD, hexagonal ports-and-adapters, event-driven inter-module communication, and configuration-as-code. Rejects simple monolithic, microservices, and serverless/FaaS as either too coupled or excessive for scope. Targets sub-100ms p99 latency, 10k+ concurrent ops, 99.9% availability, and SOC2 Type II compliance.

adrs.title: Technology Stack Selection
adrs.summary: Selects a stack with strong static typing, performance, and Phenotype-ecosystem fit. Covers type-safe config, structured logging/metrics/tracing, serialization/validation, caching, containerized deploy, modern orchestration, and CI/CD. Rejects cutting-edge experimental libs (immature), minimalist stdlib-only (delivery risk), and legacy stacks (technical debt).

adrs.title: Data Persistence Strategy
adrs.summary: Polyglot persistence: relational primary store for ACID + referential integrity, in-memory cache for hot data (sub-10ms), optional append-only event store for audit/event sourcing, read replicas, and cold archive. Lifecycle: hot/warm/cold tiers with soft-delete + audit. Rejects single document store, pure event sourcing, key-value-only, and graph DB as either too weak or over-scoped.

adrs.title: Error Handling and Recovery Strategy
adrs.summary: Explicit hierarchical error taxonomy (DomainError, InfrastructureError, SystemError, UserError) classified by origin/severity/recoverability. Principles: fail-fast, clear messages, context preservation, no silent failures, graceful degradation. Recovery: exponential backoff retry, circuit breakers, cache fallback, operator escalation. Standardized JSON error envelope with code/message/details/request_id/timestamp. Rejects exceptions-as-flow-control, bare error codes, and panic-on-everything.

adrs.title: Integration and API Design
adrs.summary: Multi-protocol: RESTful HTTP/JSON as primary public API with OAuth2/JWT, gRPC for high-perf internal service-to-service (protobuf contracts, bidi streaming, service mesh), and async event messaging (at-least-once, DLQs, schema evolution) plus webhooks. URL versioning /api/v{N}/ with 6-month deprecation window. Standardized data+meta response envelope with pagination. Rejects GraphQL-only, SOAP/XML, and custom binary protocols.

themes: Phenotype ecosystem alignment is the dominant selection criterion across every ADR (stack, persistence, integration).
Hexagonal/layered architecture + DDD + ports-and-adapters is the foundational shape; all other decisions flow from domain-logic isolation.

Polyglot-by-design: multiple protocols (REST/gRPC/events), multiple storage tiers (DB/cache/event store/archive), and multiple error categories — uniformity achieved through typed interfaces and standardized envelopes.
Observability is non-negotiable: every ADR repeats structured logging, distributed tracing, metrics, and alerting as first-class requirements.
Operational resilience patterns recur: circuit breakers, exponential backoff, graceful degradation, retry-with-jitter, health checks.
All ADRs explicitly reject three alternatives in a parallel structure, suggesting a templated/boilerplate origin rather than hand-authored decisions.
open_questions: All five ADRs use the literal string '$(date +%Y-%m-%d)' in Last Updated and Changelog fields — shell placeholders were never expanded. Real decision dates are unrecorded; provenance and author are unverifiable.
ADR-002 defers primary-language selection ('Determined by project requirements and ecosystem alignment') and ADR-001 defers concrete technology stack — what actually got built?
ADR-003 marks the event store as 'Optional' — is it in scope for argisroute, and if so, which events flow through it?
ADR-001 states argisroute is part of the Phenotype ecosystem, but does not name sibling services or shared infra (DB clusters, message bus, identity provider) it depends on.

The templated structure, identical 'Additional Implementation Considerations' / 'Decision Validation Criteria' / 'Future Considerations' sections copied verbatim across ADR-001, 002, and 003, and unrendered date placeholders suggest these were auto-generated stubs, not real deliberations. Do they reflect the team's actual reasoning, or were they backfilled from a template?

ADR-004 rejects exception-based handling 'because explicit error types provide better documentation and force handling' — which language is this targeting, since Go/Rust/Zig all use return-value errors while Java/JS/Python default to exceptions?
No ADR addresses deployment topology (single-region vs multi-region, active-active vs active-passive), data residency, or disaster-recovery RTO/RPO targets.
